paper_title: Structured Causal Video Reasoning via Multi-Objective Alignment

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Human understanding of video dynamics is typically grounded in a structured mental representation of entities, actions, and temporal relations, rather than relying solely on immediate deductive reasoning. In contrast, existing Video-LLMs largely depend on unstructured video reasoning, where critical visual evidence is embedded in verbose textual descriptions and temporal causality is often weakly modeled. This leads to inefficient processes and fragile causal inference. To bridge this cognitive gap, we propose constructing a compact representation of salient events and their causal relationships, which we name Structured Event Facts, prior to the reasoning stage. This structured prior serves as an explicit constraint to promote concise and causally grounded reasoning, while also making intermediate evidence easier to verify. To effectively train models on such structured facts, we introduce CausalFact-60K and a four-stage training pipeline comprising facts alignment, format warm-start, thinking warm-start, and reinforcement learning-based post-training. During RL stage, we find that this framework introduces competing objectives, as structural completeness and causal fidelity must be balanced against reasoning length, making it difficult to optimize. We address this challenge by formulating the optimization as a Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) problem and explicitly optimizing toward the Pareto-Frontier to balance these trade-offs. As a result, we introduce Factum-4B, which yields more reliable reasoning and delivers stronger performance on challenging video understanding tasks requiring fine-grained temporal inference.

sections.content: Structured Causal Video Reasoning via Multi-Objective Alignment Zinuo Li 1 2 Yongxin Guo 3 Jun Liu 2 Jiawei Zhan 2 Xi Jiang 4 Chengjie Wang 2 Mohammed Bennamoun 1 Farid Boussaid 1 Feng Zheng 4 Qiuhong Ke 5 6 2 0 2 6 ] . [ 1 5 1 4 4 0 . 4 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Human understanding of video dynamics is typically grounded in structured mental representation of entities, actions, and temporal relations, rather than relying solely on immediate deductive reasoning. In contrast, existing Video-LLMs largely depend on unstructured video reasoning, where critical visual evidence is embedded in verbose textual descriptions and temporal causality is often weakly modeled. This leads to inefficient processes and fragile causal inference. To bridge this cognitive gap, we propose constructing compact representation of salient events and their causal relationships, which we name Structured Event Facts, prior to the reasoning stage. This structured prior serves as an explicit constraint to promote concise and causally grounded reasoning, while also making intermediate evidence easier to verify. To effectively train models on such structured facts, we introduce CausalFact60K and four-stage training pipeline comprising facts alignment, format warm-start, thinking warm-start, and reinforcement learning-based post-training. During RL stage, we find that this framework introduces competing objectives, as structural completeness and causal fidelity must be balanced against reasoning length, making it difficult to optimize. We address this challenge by formulating the optimization as Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) problem and explicitly optimizing toward the Pareto-Frontier to balance these trade-offs. As result, we introduce Factum-4B, which yields more reliable reasoning and delivers stronger performance on challenging video understanding tasks requiring fine-grained temporal inference. This work was conducted during the internship of Zinuo 1University of Western AusLi at Tencent Youtu Lab. tralia 2Tencent Youtu Lab 3Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shen Zhen 4Southern University of Science and Technology 5Monash University. Correspondence to: Qiuhong Ke <qiuhong.ke@monash.edu>. Preprint. April 7, 2026. 1 Figure 1. Compared with existing video reasoning approaches, our model first extracts facts event information from videos. It then applies thinking process strictly constrained by causal relationships, which is specifically optimized for video data. This method clarifies critical information and enhances interpretability while focusing on the temporal dimension of videos. Video is sampled from ActivityNet-Captions (Krishna et al., 2017). 1. Introduction Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable success in interpreting static images by adapting the architectural paradigms of Large Language Models (LLMs) (Liu et al., 2024a; Bai et al., 2023). Recently, this progress has extended to the video domain, aiming to capture dynamic temporal information (Zhang et al., 2023a; Li et al., 2024). To enhance the reasoning capabilities of these models, the research community has predominantly adopted the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) paradigm (Wei et al., 2022) in Large Language Models, which encourages models to generate intermediate reasoning steps before deriving final answer. Later on, CoT has also been proven effective to multimodal tasks (Bai et al., 2025a). Unlike text, video data is characterized by dense spatiotemporal redundancy. When models simply employ unstructured CoT like text-only tasks, the resulting generation often devolves into verbose, non-linear narratives where pivotal visual cues are drowned in irrelevant tokens. Empirically, we observe that reasoning-enhanced Thinking models often degrade in performance compared to their Instructiontuned counterparts (Bai et al., 2025a). They suffer from reasoning drift, losing focus on the user query, and frequently reduce the temporal dimension to isolated frame retrieval rather than analyzing causal relationships. This behavior contrasts sharply with human cognitive processes. Psychological research suggests that human perception relies on forming structured mental sketch, which registers entities, actions and event boundaries before engaging in abstract reasoning (Zacks & Swallow, 2007). Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Motivated by this cognitive mechanism, we propose Structure-First paradigm, module generated prior to the reasoning phase. As shown in Figure 1, we enforce the model to synthesize high-density schema containing time, object, human action, scene, and causal event caption before the reasoning process begins. This structured prior serves as an explicit constraint, anchoring the subsequent reasoning to be concise, salient, and causally grounded. However, there is often trade-off between maintaining structural strictness and ensuring reasoning length. We find that this multi-stage reasoning process presents non-trivial optimization challenge during Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage. In resource-constrained settings with limited length budgets, maximizing facts density can inadvertently truncate reasoning depth, and vice versa. Standard RL strategies (Christiano et al., 2017), typically optimized via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), or Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) (Shao et al., 2024) struggle to balance these conflicting rewards, leading to training instability, where the advantages calculation cannot distinguish optimization direction. To avoid collapsing multiple rewards into single scalar, which can obscure critical trade-offs, we treat multidimensional reward vectors as distinct signals for policy updates. Unlike standard GRPO, we treat each reward component as an independent objective to preserve taskspecific gradients. Inspired by the Multiple Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) (Desideri, 2012), we propose Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB) algorithm to effectively resolve rewards conflicts and dynamically balance distinct objectives. Our contributions are summarized as follows: We identify the limitations of unstructured CoT in video understanding. To address this, we propose CausalFact-60K, dataset that empowers the model to precisely output facts information and utilize causal relationships for video reasoning with dense captions. We reveal that the standard GRPO advantage calculation may lead to conflicts among multi-objective rewards and ambiguous gradient directions. Motivated by the pareto-frontier, we propose Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB) algorithm. This algorithm dynamically balances multi-objective advantages based on pareto-optimal during the advantage calculation process. Based on aforementioned contributions, we train small but strong model: Factum-4B. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that Factum-4B significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating the effectiveness of structured reasoning process and P-FAB algorithm. 2. Related Work Video understanding LVLMs. Following the success of image-to-text LVLMs (Liu et al., 2024a; Dai et al., 2023), early video LLMs treated videos as sequences of static frames and employed token compression to fit highdimensional spatio-temporal signals into limited LLM token budgets (Maaz et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023a). However, this frame-level approach overlooks core challenges in video understanding, particularly temporal grounding and causal reasoning (Liu et al., 2024c; Chen et al., 2024). To address this, recent works have introduced time-aware architectures (Ren et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2024; 2025) to better capture temporal dynamics, or incorporated memory and tool-augmented mechanisms for handling long-form, untrimmed videos (Song et al., 2024; Qian et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). Parallel efforts focus on improving training data quality and scale (Zhang et al., 2024) and establishing reliable benchmarks for evaluating long-horizon temporal and causal reasoning (Mangalam et al., 2023; Fu et al., 2024). Yet, despite architectural advances, current video LLMs often rely on sparse frame heuristics rather than modeling true event dynamics (Mangalam et al., 2023; Fu et al., 2024; Xiao et al., 2021), limiting their performance in complex real-world scenarios. Our method addresses this by learning an explicit structured prior that summarizes key events and their causal relationships before free-form reasoning, effectively enhancing temporal grounding and reducing dependence on brittle frame-selection shortcuts. Reasoning and chain-of-thought in LVLMs. Chain-ofThought (CoT) prompting (Wei et al., 2022) improves LLM reasoning by eliciting intermediate steps, and MultimodalCoT extends this to vision-language models by conditioning reasoning traces on visual features (Zhang et al., 2023b). However, visual inputs are often both redundant, containing many irrelevant frames, also underspecified with finegrained temporal and causal cues easily missed. As result, unconstrained vision Chain of Thought tends to become verbose, drift off-topic, or hallucinate unsupported details, especially when intermediate steps lack grounding in verifiable evidence (Chen et al., 2023). Prior approaches address this through tool-augmented verification (e.g., ReAct/MMReAct) (Yao et al., 2023b; Yang et al., 2023), exploration of multiple reasoning paths (e.g., Tree-of-Thoughts, selfconsistency) (Yao et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2023). In contrast, we generate an internal Structured Event Facts, compact, high-density schema encoding key events, entities, and causal relationships, which explicitly constrains subsequent CoT reasoning to be concise, salient, and firmly grounded in extracted video facts. Reinforcement learning in LVLMs Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) aligns LLMs 2 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Figure 2. The complete reasoning pipeline of our model. Given video, we first establish event-level facts information. This step highlights all critical clues, such as time, person, and human action. These clues constrain the subsequent thinking process, enabling the model to reason logically based on evidence while focusing on temporal causal relationships. Video is sampled from ActivityNet-Captions (Krishna et al., 2017). with human preferences using comparison-based feedback (Ouyang & et al., 2022; Touvron & et al., 2023), typically via scalar rewards optimized with PPO (Schulman et al., 2017). Recent work proposes GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), more efficient alternative that updates policies using group-wise relative comparisons, reducing reliance on value-function estimation while enabling rule-based rewards modeling. For LVLMs, existing approaches directly apply GRPO to diverse video understanding tasks such as reasoning, temporal grounding, and counting (Feng et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025). However, these methods overlook the inherently multi-objective nature of video understanding, where models must balance objectives including structural constraints, reasoning capability, final accuracy, and computational budgets, etc. In this paper, rather than naively averaging rewards, we optimize reward weights by solving Multi-Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) objective to approximate the Pareto-Frontier (Kong et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025b). This approach ensures fair, stable, and balanced optimization across all objectives. 3. Methodology 3.1. Overview To reformulate complex video thinking, straightforward approach would typically involve two-stage process: Thinking Warm-start to establish causal logic, followed by Reinforcement Learning for alignment. However, the unique integration of Structured Event Facts within our framework necessitates specialized orchestration of the training steps. We empirically find that this facts-first dependency introduces an optimization barrier. Directly optimizing accurate factual description and complex causal reasoning through these two simple stages overwhelms the model, leading to hallucinated facts or collapsed reasoning structures. To resolve this conflict, we decouple the learning process into four progressive stages: Facts Training (Stage 1), Format Warm-Start (Stage 1.5), Thinking Warm-Start (Stage 2), and RL-based Post-training (Stage 3). Stage 1 trains the model to produce high-quality facts outputs with accurate descriptions of person, action, scene, etc. Stage 2 focuses on training the models causal reasoning ability. These two stages are both trained through instruction tuning. In Stage 1, we ask the model to output facts information from the video, while in Stage 2, we ask real QA questions such as temporal grounding and visual reasoning. Detailed prompts are shown in the Appendix B.4. Notably, to bridge the gap between pure factual description (Stage 1) and complex reasoning (Stage 2), we inIn this stage, we troduce an intermediate Stage 1.5. ask for the same facts as in Stage 1 but require the model to strictly adhere to the thinking format (e.g., <thinking>...<answering>), thereby warming up the structural format. Otherwise, the model hallucinates bad structure. Finally, Stage 3 employs our proposed ParetoFrontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB) to resolve the objective conflicts in multi-objective reinforcement learning. Since each stage requires distinct supervision signals, we construct comprehensive data curation pipeline, detailed in Section 3.2. Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 3.2. Data Curation: Facts and Thinking 3.2.1. MOTIVATION Although video reasoning has raised researchers attention, existing video understanding models often reason without structure (Wang et al., 2025; Bai et al., 2025a), where critical clues are submerged in redundant text. Conventional Chain-of-Thought (CoT) can become verbose and unfocused on long videos, introducing invalid steps while underemphasizing temporal causal logic in favor of discrete time points. This motivates us to propose thinking process with clearer clues, better interpretability, and stronger causality. Thus, we propose to model the video understanding problem as follows: Facts, Thinking, Answering, where clear reasoning process with strong causality can be established. Specifically, we force the model to output Structured Event Facts as the primary step of our methodology to enhance structured information extraction, aiming to provide precise, compact, and query-relevant clues that establish reliable semantic foundation for subsequent reasoning. In this stage, the model is required to describe the major events in the video at reasonable intervals, spanning approximately 10 to 30 seconds, and extract the key cues within each event, such as salient characters, human actions, scene context, and camera position, and then provide detailed, time-ordered description of each event in chronological sequence, as illustrated Figure 2. Building upon the resulting Structured Event Facts, we aim to steer the model toward better causal reasoning. Leveraging the salient clues extracted in the facts phase, we enforce structured causal reasoning process with three tightly coupled stages: 1. Global Search & Localization, 2. Causal Verification, and 3. Final Alignment. As shown in Figure 2. The first phase Global Search & Localization requires the model to retrieve query-relevant clues from the structured facts list to quickly narrow attention to small set of highly relevant time windows, providing reliable starting point for downstream reasoning. In Causal Verification stage, it examines temporal coherence by analyzing the preceding and succeeding events around the hypothesized interval, and validates the localization through cause, which effects consistency rather than shallow pattern matching. To avoid over-reliance on textual priors and keep the decision grounded in visual evidence, the model performs mandatory visual verification on the inferred interval using vision tokens sent with textual prompts. In Final Alignment stage, the model performs global consistency check to ensure the inferred temporal interval is fully contained within the observed activity sequences, improving the overall reliability of the reasoning process. Figure 3. Overview of the two-stage pipeline for constructing from VTG datasets. Stage 1 performs video filtering and gap filling, generates structured facts captions, and applies an automatic quality judge with random human inspection; low-quality samples are rejected and iteratively refined. Stage 2 produces causally grounded reasoning traces, followed by second quality-judging and human spot-checking step, yielding the final curated causalfacts set. 3.2.2. DATA CONSTRUCTION PIPELINE As illustrated in Figure 3, to enable high-quality facts and Thinking generation, we build comprehensive data construction pipeline. For pre-processing, we adopt strict selection pipeline to distill informative samples from high-quality humanannotated Video Temporal Grounding datasets (Gao et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2017). More video source details are shown in the Appendix B. We prioritize videos with high event density, assuming an ideal event segment spans 20 30 seconds: for video of duration , we retain it only if event number /30, i.e., 150-second video requires at least 5 events. For retained videos, we further apply gap filling to improve continuity: unlabeled intervals between adjacent annotated events (e.g., unlabeled [25, 38] between annotated [15, 25] and [38, 50]) are treated as candidate event segments for the model to describe, increasing coverage and encouraging global spatiotemporal modeling. Videos with <T /30 are excluded from facts training to avoid weakening structured signals, but are retained for the subsequent RL stage to enhance robustness and generalization. Next, we feed the filtered dense timestamps into facts Generator to produce the event facts descriptions. Since facts are fine-grained structured signals that require strong instruction following to achieve high data quality, we employ two complementary models: Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025a) and Gemini2.5Pro (Comanici et al., 2025). To prevent specific model preferences from compromising data quality, the two models alternate roles: when one serves as the generator, the other acts as quality judge. The two strong models mutually critique and filter outputs, enabling facts to capture detailed visual cues. The data produced in this stage is served for Stage 1 and Stage 1.5. After obtaining the facts, we generate challenging questions and prompt the LLM to produce explicit thinking traces conditioned on the question and the associated facts information. Since thinking data demands higher quality than facts itself, we use Gemini2.5-Pro exclusively as the gen4 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 vm as directional preference vector, in essence vote from objective on which samples to promote or suppress. By stacking these update signals into matrix and solving for the minimum-norm combination within its convex hull, we derive compromise update that is maximally compatible with all objectives. Inspired by MGDA (Desideri, 2012), we propose Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB) algorithm, as described in Algorithm 1. Figure 4. GRPO vs P-FAB advantage comparison. P-FAB dynamically adjusts weights by solving minimum-norm problem in the standardized reward space, ensuring that rare but critical signals are not overwhelmed by high-variance conflicting objectives. Algorithm 1 P-FAB 1: Input: 2: RN : rewards for responses and erator in this stage, while Qwen3-VL serves as the quality judge. The data produced in this stage is served for Stage 2. To ensure data quality, we conduct human evaluation by sampling 50% of the total 60K annotations. Specifically, we partition the generated data into batches of 1,000 instances and randomly select 500 samples from each batch for manual inspection. 3.3. Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing Motivations. After the first three stages, the model acquires structured reasoning capabilities. In Stage 3, we introduce reinforcement learning to align the model with real-world problem-solving. However, the integration of facts, thinking, and answering components naturally elongates the chain-of-thought, transforming the training into complex Multi-Objective Optimization problem, where two problems may occur in standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO): (1) Inherent Conflicts: There is natural Pareto trade-off between comprehensive fact extraction and token efficiency, which static weights cannot dynamically navigate; and (2) Directional Ambiguity: Standard GRPO cannot distinguish update differences between multiple objectives. As illustrated in Figure 4, GRPO produces identical advantage values for candidates with the same average rewards, thereby masking the underlying distribution and potential imbalances among different reward components. Pitfalls of traditional policy gradient optimization. In policy gradient optimization, updates follow the direction θ log πθ(oi qi), where the advantage is derived from weighted reward Rfinal = (cid:80) wiri. To manage conflicting objectives, common practice is to tune the coefficients wi to prioritize specific goals. However, since these weights remain static during training, they often exacerbate tradeoffs rather than resolving the underlying conflicts. P-FAB as solution. We reformulate this interaction by defining group-wide reward vector vm = [r1,m, . . . , rG,m] RG for each objective m, where is the number of responses per prompt. We interpret each objectives RN : scalarized advantage for each sample ZN : group ID for each response τ > 0: standard deviation threshold (e.g., 106) // Step 1: Rewards Centralization (Eq. 1) Iq {i : gi = q} 3: 4: 5: Output: 6: 7: Initialize 0N 8: for each unique group unique(g) do 9: 10: 11: Dq RIq,: mean(RIq,:, axis = 0) 12: 13: 14: 15: 16: 17: 18: 19: // Step 2: Standardized Conflict Resolution (Eq. 3) σq std(Dq, axis = 0) {m : σq,m > τ = 1e 6} Valid objectives if = then α 1 Standardization = arg minα ˆDqα2 s.t. α Fallback to uniform Indices in group 1M else ˆDq Dq[:, V]/σq[V] Solve: α via Frank-Wolfe α 0M ; α[V] α end if // Step 3: Advantage Scalarization (Eq. 4) Dqα Apply weights to centered rewards mean(Araw ))/(std(Araw ) + ϵ) 20: 21: 22: 23: Araw 24: A[Iq] (Araw 25: end for 26: return Methodology Details. Following the group-relative approach in GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), we first isolate the relative quality of each completion within its prompt group. Consider batch containing prompts, where each prompt generates group of responses {o1, . . . , oG}. For each objective m, the centered reward δi,m for response oi is computed by subtracting the group mean: δi,m = rm(q, oi) 1 (cid:88) j=1 rm(q, oj). (1) This normalization ensures that δi,m indicates whether response oi outperforms the group average for objective m. Solving Common Direction. The theoretical foundation of our approach stems from MGDA. In multi-objective op5 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 timization, the goal is to find parameter update that simultaneously improves all objectives {L1(ω), . . . , LM (ω)}. MGDA achieves this by seeking convex combination of task gradients that forms common descent direction. key result from MGDA theory shows that finding such direction is equivalent to solving the following dual problem: min αM (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:88) m=1 αmLm(ω) (cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) . (2) When the optimal norm is zero, Pareto-stationary point is reached, otherwise, the minimizing combination yields descent direction that benefits all tasks. In our preference optimization setting, the centered reward δi,m defined in Eq. (1) plays an analogous role to task gradients: it represents the per-sample, per-objective signal indicating how the policy should be updated. This analogy allows us to adapt the MGDA principle to our setting: by minimizing the norm of the weighted combination (cid:80) αmδi,m, we seek consensus direction that balances all reward objectives. However, primary challenge in applying MGDA directly to δi,m is scale sensitivity: objectives with high variance can dominate the norm minimization, suppressing signals from other tasks. To ensure scale invariance, P-FAB computes optimal weights in standardized space. For prompt group q, let Dq be the matrix with entries δi,m. We construct the standardized matrix ˆDq by scaling each column by its standard deviation σq,m, ignoring objectives where σq,m < τ = 1e 6 to maintain numerical stability. Next, we seek the weight vector α by employing the FrankWolfe algorithm (detailed in Appendix D.2), which is highly efficient for quadratic optimization to minimize the norm of the aggregate direction within this standardized space: α = arg min αM s.t. = {α RM (cid:13) ˆDqα (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:88) . αm = 1, αm 0} (3) Advantage scalarization. Once the Pareto-optimal weights α are obtained, we apply them to the centered rewards Dq to preserve the original signal magnitude fidelity, followed by final group-wise normalization: Araw = (cid:88) m=1 α qi,m δi,m, Ai = Araw µA σA + ϵ . (4) the scalarized advantage Ai is substituted Finally, Let ρi(θ) = into the standard GRPO objective. πθ(oiqi)/πθold(oiqi) be the probability ratio. The policy is optimized by minimizing: L(θ) = 1 (cid:80)N i=1 [min (ρi(θ)Ai, clip(ρi(θ), 1 ε, 1 + ε)Ai) βDKL] (5) where ε is the clipping parameter to prevent excessive policy updates, and βDKL is the KL-divergence penalty ensuring the policy stays close to the reference model. Discussion on P-FAB. As illustrated in Figure 4, P-FAB 1) naturally amplifies sparse and hard-to-satisfy objectives while reducing the weights of rewards that are more easily obtained. Consequently, the resulting advantage values are more discriminative, prioritizing samples that satisfy critical, rare criteria over those that merely perform well on simpler tasks. 2) We consider the model to have reached local Pareto-Frontier when the objectives achieve Pareto stationarity. In this state, the convex hull of the task gradients contains the origin (minα (cid:80) αmLm 0), implying that no common descent direction exists. At this point, any further improvement in one objective necessitates degradation in another, signifying that the model has fully navigated the inherent trade-offs of the task. 4. Experiments 4.1. Implementation Details We use Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct as our base model. Since the 4B model is more susceptible to general-capability forgetting, we adopt stage-specific hyperparameter settings across our three training stages. For Stage 1, Stage 1.5, and Stage 2, we fine-tune the model with LoRA, but with different hyperparameters. In Stage 1 and Stage 1.5, we use LoRA with rank 64 and α = 128, learning rate of 1 105, and weight decay of 1 104 to mitigate catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. In Stage 2, we keep the same LoRA setup but increase the learning rate to 1 104 and weight decay to 2 104. For the RL Stage 3, we use 4 different rewards: Format, Linear IoU, Multi-choice Accuracy, and Length, where Length reward makes the conflicts. Detailed reward designs are shown in the Appendix D.1. We perform full-parameter updates with learning rate of 5 106, num generation = 8, video max tokens set to 64. We set video fps = 1.0 and epoch = 1.0 across all stages, with video max frames = 256 for Stage1, Stage1.5 and Stage2, while deceasing to video max frames = 128 for Stage3 to save memory. During the evaluation, we re-evaluate Qwen3 series based on the same settings of ours, i.e., fps = 1 and max frames = 256. For other models, their results come from the official reports, typically with fps = 2 and max frames = 2048. 4.2. Ablation Studies We evaluate component effectiveness on CharadesTimeLens (Zhang et al., 2025a) and ActivityNetCaptions (Krishna et al., 2017) for temporal grounding, and VideoMME (Fu et al., 2024) and MLVU (Zhou et al., 2025) Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Model Charades-TimeLens ActivityNet-Captions VideoMME MLVU R1@0.3 R1@0.5 R1@0.7 R1@0.3 R1@0.5 R1@0.7 Acc (w/o sub) TR (Acc) Ego (Acc) Baseline Models 1fps Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct* (Bai et al., 2025a) Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking* (Bai et al., 2025a) 56.5 56.3 37.8 37.4 18.4 17.8 50.2 47.8 35.8 31.7 21.6 18. 63.9 63.1 80.4 79.5 60.3 59.0 Ablations 1fps 16.5 w/o Facts 56.8 55.4 16.1 w/o Thinking 18.2 w/o RL 57.5 19.4 GRPO = 4 58.9 59.4 20.1 P-FAB = 4 20.4 GRPO = 8 59.8 20.6 GDPO=8 59.2 Factum-4B 21.6 60.3 Table 1. Ablation studies on two classical video temporal grounding datasets and two general understanding datasets. TR and Ego mean Topic Reasoning and Egocentric Video Understanding, respectively. 54.0 53.1 55.4 56.4 56.3 57.8 57.2 57.1 76.2 75.6 76.8 78.5 79.1 79.3 79.8 80. 58.8 55.6 61.5 64.9 66.1 67.3 67.7 69.8 37.0 36.2 38.2 39.1 39.4 39.6 39.2 40.4 23.2 21.8 23.7 24.6 25.2 26.7 25.3 28.1 60.8 58.5 59.1 60.2 62.7 63.2 63.5 64.7 41.5 40.4 41.6 43.1 45.1 45.2 45.7 48.4 for general understanding. We compare our full model against five variants: (1) w/o Facts excludes factual descriptions to perform direct structured thinking; (2) w/o Thinking generates facts but predicts answers without causal reasoning; (3) w/o RL utilizes only supervised fine-tuning; and (4-5) GRPO/P-FAB, comparing standard GRPO against our P-FAB with num generation = 4 or 8. Detailed dataset introductions are shown in the Appendix A. As shown in Table 1, both of the facts information and structured thinking are indispensable. Removing the thinking process (w/o Thinking) leads to the most severe performance collapse across all benchmarks, notably dropping to 58.5% on VideoMME. This failure stems from the models inability to effectively utilize the extracted facts. Without reasoning bridge, the causal link between factual perception and the final answer is severed, rendering the facts underutilized and disconnected from the objective. Similarly, omitting structured facts (w/o Facts) significantly impairs performance, as these facts serve as clear cues that guide the thinking process. Without this grounding foundation, the reasoning chain frequently enters logical pitfalls or misguided paths, confirming that verified facts act as critical anchor to steer the thinking process away from misconceptions. Regarding the optimization strategy, our P-FAB consistently outperforms standard GRPO. Crucially, while P-FAB already shows clear gains at group size of 4, the performance gap between P-FAB and standard GRPO widens significantly when the group size is increased to 8. For instance, on ActivityNet R1@0.3, the margin between P-FAB (our full model) and GRPO grows from 1.2% at G=4 to 2.5% at G=8. This suggests that P-FAB possesses superior optimization dynamics in larger group settings, where it can more effectively navigate the expanded sampling space to resolve complex multi-objective conflicts and more fully manifest its advantages. Finally, our RL post-training yields substantial 8.3% gain on ActivityNet R1@0.3 compared to the w/o RL baseline, with our full model achieving state-ofthe-art results across both temporal grounding and general reasoning tasks. 4.3. Comparisons with Existing Video-LLMs We evaluate Factum-4B against state-of-the-art close-source and open-source Video-LLMs across both temporal grounding and general video understanding benchmarks. As summarized in Table 2 and Table 3, our model demonstrates significant performance leap, particularly in fine-grained temporal reasoning and causal deduction. Leading Performance in Temporal Grounding. Factum4B sets new state-of-the-art among open-source models for temporal localization. Despite operating at lower frame rate (1fps), it consistently outperforms 7B-scale models such as Time-R1-7B and VideoChat-R1-7B. On ActivityNetCaptions, our model achieves substantial R1@0.5 of 48.4% and R1@0.7 of 28.1%, significantly surpassing Time-R1-7B. Furthermore, on the high-precision CharadesTimeLens benchmark, Factum-4B reaches an R1@0.7 of 21.6%, the highest among all open-source candidates. Compared to Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking, our model improves the R1@0.3 on ActivityNet-TimeLens from 48.2% to 54.7%, validating that our P-FAB reinforcement learning stage effectively refines the models ability to ground reasoning in precise temporal boundaries. Superior Reasoning in General Video Understanding. The advantages of our structured-fact-guided thinking extend to broader semantic tasks. On ETBench, Factum-4B leads in six 6/8 sub-tasks, notably achieving 66.1% in TVG task and 26.8% in TEM task. Remarkably, our 4B model even surpasses the proprietary GPT-4o in these specific temporal-heavy tasks, proving that structured thinking process not only improves temporal abilities, but also improves general understanding performances. This trend continues across comprehensive benchmarks: Factum-4B achieves 7 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Model Close-Source Models 2fps GPT-4o (Hurst et al., 2024) GPT-5 (OpenAI, 2025) Gemini-2.0-Flash (Comanici et al., 2025) Gemini-2.5-Flash (Comanici et al., 2025) Gemini-2.5-Pro (Comanici et al., 2025) Open-Source Models 2fps VideoChat-R1-7B (Li et al., 2025a) TRACE-7B (Guo et al., 2024) Time-R1-7B (Wang et al., 2025) Qwen2.5-VL-7B (Bai et al., 2025b) Charades-TimeLens ActivityNet-TimeLens ActivityNet-Captions R1@0.3 R1@0.5 R1@0.7 R1@0.3 R1@0.5 R1@0.7 R1@0.3 R1@0.5 R1@0.7 60.6 59.3 66.4 68.7 74.1 51.9 - 57.9 59. 44.5 42.0 53.5 56.1 61.1 30.8 - 32.0 37.8 23.5 22.0 27.1 30.6 34.0 11.7 - 16.9 16.6 55.2 57.4 62.9 66.8 72.3 35.0 - 44.8 44. 41.4 44.9 54.0 57.5 64.2 23.9 - 31.0 31.0 25.8 30.4 37.7 41.3 47.1 11.3 - 19.0 16.1 - - 50.4 51.2 - - 54.0 58.1 34. - - 33.2 34.7 - 33.3 37.7 39.0 20.8 - - 19.9 21.0 - 16.7 24.0 21.4 11.2 Open-Source Models 1fps Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct* (Bai et al., 2025a) Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking* (Bai et al., 2025a) Factum-4B 18.4 17.8 21.6 Table 2. Experiments on the public video temporal understanding benchmarks. * means this model is reproduced under the same video settings of our model, otherwise, the results are from official reports with more video frames. 56.5 56.3 57.1 37.8 37.4 40.4 21.6 19.0 28.1 25.2 24.9 26.7 50.2 47.8 69.8 35.8 31.7 48. 36.2 36.7 41.4 50.2 48.2 54.7 ETBench VideoMME MLVU NExT-GQA Metrics Close-Source Models 2fps GPT-4o (Hurst et al., 2024) GPT-4v (OpenAI, 2023) RAR ECA RVQ TVG EPM TAL TEM GVQ Acc Acc Acc Acc Rec F1 F1 F1 27.8 33.3 27.3 40.9 57.7 46. 40.4 27.0 4.5 1.8 20.0 18.0 13.6 23.9 - - Acc 71.9 59.9 TR (Acc) Ego (Acc) - - - - Acc - - Oepn-Source Models 1fps VideoLLama2-7B (Cheng et al., 2024) VTimeLLM-7B (Huang et al., 2024) TRACE-7B (Guo et al., 2024) TinyLLaVA-Video-R1-3B (Zhang et al., 2025b) Qwen2.5-VL-7B (Bai et al., 2025b) Qwen2.5-VL-3B (Bai et al., 2025b) Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025a) Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking (Bai et al., 2025a) Factum-4B Table 3. Experiments on the public general video understanding benchmarks. TR and Ego mean Topic Reasoning and Egocentric Video Understanding, respectively. * means this model is reproduced under the same video settings of our model, otherwise, the results are from official reports with more video frames. 0.0 18.2 21.6 - 15.3 14.8 20.6 20.0 19.7 27.4 31.0 28.8 - 42.0 47.2 54.4 51.0 53.4 28.8 28.4 29.4 - 41.0 38.4 53.1 53.0 53.4 28.0 28.0 42.6 - 51.4 52.2 54.3 54.2 55. - 1.9 52.4 - 32.7 55.1 57.7 55.6 58.3 0.1 7.6 46.8 - 20.1 19.9 64.6 64.3 66.1 0.0 1.9 12.3 - 2.9 2.6 14.8 15.7 16.8 0.0 6.9 17.8 - 22.3 18.1 9.6 20.4 26.8 - 17.4 - - 59.5 - 72.1 66.6 73.6 - - - - 50.0 - 60.3 59.0 60. - - - - 74.9 - 80.4 79.5 80.6 - - 49.6 46.6 62.9 61.5 63.9 63.1 64.7 64.7% on VideoMME and state-of-the-art 73.6% on NExTGQA, representing 7% absolute gain over the Thinking base model. On MLVU, our model maintains top-tier performance in Temporal Reasoning and Ego-centric understanding, effectively bridging the gap between open-source specialized models and multi-billion parameter proprietary systems. These combined results underscore that by enabling causal reasoning, Factum-4B achieves more robust and granular understanding of complex video dynamics. Discussion and Weaknesses. consistent trend across benchmarks is that unstructured thinking does not enable better video understanding ability: Qwen3-VL4B-Thinking consistently underperforms Qwen3-VL-4BInstruct on nearly all benchmarks, aligning with our motivation that verbose reasoning can introduce evidence drift and weaken temporal alignment. In contrast, our framework explicitly organizes important clues into compact format, leading to stronger performance and improved precision under stricter temporal thresholds. However, our current training data is still limited, theres need to scale up the dataset to enable the model to achieve strong performance across all video understanding tasks in the future. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we present Factum-4B, Video-LLM that bridges the cognitive gap between human video understanding and current models by introducing Structured Event Facts, serving as an explicit constraint that guides the model towards interpretable causal video reasoning. We develop four-stage training pipeline that progressively equips the model with facts sketching and causal reasoning towards real-world task. To handle multi-objectives trade-offs under constrained length budgets during RL, we introduce Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB) algorithm. Empirically, Factum-4B yields more stable and reliable reasoning, delivering stronger performance on challenging video understanding tasks that demand temporal localization and causal inference. We hope this work pro8 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 vides promising direction for scalable, evidence-grounded video reasoning systems.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: his paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of machine learning. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none of which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

sections.title: Appendix Contents
sections.content: A. Detailed Information of Evaluation Benchmarks To comprehensively evaluate the capabilities of our model, particularly in temporal reasoning and open-ended generation, we utilize diverse set of benchmarks categorized into two primary domains: Video Temporal Grounding and General Video Understanding. A.1. Video Temporal Grounding Benchmarks These benchmarks evaluate the models ability to precisely localize specific moments within untrimmed videos based on textual queries. Charades-TimeLens (Zhang et al., 2025a): high-quality dataset re-annotated based on the Charades-STA dataset, where 66.3% of low-quality annotations were rewritten. The final dataset contains 1,313 videos and 3,363 annotations, with an average video duration of 29.3 seconds. This benchmark focuses on daily indoor activities (e.g., cooking, reading) and is characterized by dense actions and complex temporal overlaps. Unlike the noisy annotations in the original dataset, Charades-TimeLens provides rigorously verified timestamps to evaluate fine-grained temporal localization. We report Recall@1 at IoU thresholds of 0.3, 0.5, and 0.7. ActivityNet-TimeLens (Zhang et al., 2025a): re-annotated version of the ActivityNet-Captions (Krishna et al., 2017) dataset introduced in the TimeLens benchmark, where 69.7% of low-quality annotations were rewritten. The final dataset contains 1455 videos and 4500 annoations, with and average video duration of 134.9s. It addresses the issue of loose boundaries in the original annotations by providing stricter and more precise start/end times for temporal queries. Evaluating on this split offers more rigorous assessment of models boundary alignment capabilities. ActivityNet-Captions (Krishna et al., 2017): large-scale Video Temporal Grounding benchmark containing 4885 open-domain YouTube videos for evaluation, with 17031 annotations and an average video duration of 118s. It covers wide range of complex human activities. The dataset is widely used for dense video captioning and temporal grounding. We evaluate performance using Recall@1 at IoU thresholds of 0.3, 0.5, and 0.7. A.2. General Video Understanding Benchmarks These benchmarks assess the models holistic understanding, including temporal logic, causal reasoning, and ego-centric perception. VideoMME (Fu et al., 2024): comprehensive Multi-modal LLM Evaluation benchmark designed for long-form video understanding. It covers diverse domains including movies, sports, and documentaries, with video lengths ranging from minutes to hours. It assesses the models ability to process long-context information without subtitles. MLVU (Zhou et al., 2025): holistic benchmark specifically tailored for long videos. It includes diverse tasks such as Topic Reasoning, Anomaly Recognition, Egocentric Video Understanding QA, etc. In our experiments, we specifically focus on the Temporal Reasoning (TR) and Egocentric Video Understanding (Ego) sub-tasks to evaluate the models sequence understanding and first-person perspective reasoning. ETBench (Liu et al., 2024b): fine-grained benchmark designed to evaluate time-sensitive video understanding. We test the model on 8 different tasks: Referred Action Recognition (RAR), Event-Caption Alignment (ECA), Refferred Video Question-Answering (RVQ), Temporal Video Grounding (TVG), Episodic Memory (EPM), Temporal Action Localization (TAL), Temporal Event Matching (TEM), and Grounded Video Question-Answering (GVQ). This benchmark is crucial for verifying whether the models thinking process translates to accurate event-level deductions. NExT-GQA (Xiao et al., 2024): An extension of the NExT-QA dataset that introduces grounded video question answering. It focuses on causal (Why) and temporal (How) questions involving rich object interactions. Unlike standard QA, NExT-GQA requires the model to not only answer the question but also ground the answer in specific video segments, making it an ideal testbed for our reasoning-based approach. 12 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 B. More details of CausalFacts-60K B.1. Video Source Distribution The distribution of video sources utilized in our framework is presented in Figure 5. We curate total of 32,049 videos from diverse high-quality benchmarks to construct robust training corpus, all the videos are only selected from the training splits of the original datasets, and no original annotations are used. As shown in the statistics, the dataset is predominantly anchored by ActivityNet (Krishna et al., 2017), providing broad coverage of general open-domain activities. This is complemented by significant subsets from QVHighlights (Lei et al., 2021) and COIN (Tang et al., 2019), which enhance the models capability in saliency detection and fine-grained procedural understanding, respectively. Additionally, Charades-STA (Gao et al., 2017) contributes essential data for indoor daily actions, while Molmo2 (Clark et al., 2026) and YouCookII (Zhou et al., 2018) provide small number of supplementary samples. To ensure the reliability of temporal reasoning, we select these sources specifically for their rigorous human-annotated temporal boundaries. We strictly preserve these gold-standard timestamps to guarantee precise event localization. For each validated time interval, we discard the original unstructured captions and regenerate high-density structured descriptions. This approach effectively combines the temporal precision of human annotations with the semantic richness required for our structured reasoning tasks. Figure 5. Distribution of Video Sources. Our dataset comprises 32,049 videos selected from high-quality VTG benchmarks. We utilize the precise human-annotated timestamps from these sources while regenerating the textual content to align with our structured event schema. B.2. Video Duration and Topic Distribution In this section, we provide additional statistical details for the CausalFact-60K dataset to supplement the main paper. The dataset contains total of = 32, 049 video samples. Video Duration. As illustrated in Figure 6 (Left), the dataset covers wide range of video lengths. The average duration is 109.4 seconds, with median of 123.6 seconds. The distribution is concentrated around the 1 to 3-minute range, ensuring that the videos contain sufficient temporal context for event unfolding while remaining concise enough for efficient processing. Topic Distribution. Figure 6 (Right) presents the distribution across 18 distinct semantic categories. The dataset is primarily composed of event-rich topics such as Tutorials and Sports, which inherently contain structured actions and clear causal dependencies. Furthermore, the inclusion of diverse categories like Vlog, Daily Life, Cooking, and Travel ensures the semantic diversity of the dataset, covering both structured instructional content and open-ended daily scenarios. 13 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Figure 6. Statistics of the CausalFact-60K dataset. Left: The histogram of video durations, showing mean length of 109.4s and median of 123.6s. Right: The frequency distribution of 18 semantic topics, dominated by action-intensive categories such as Tutorials and Sports. B.3. RL Training Data Details In this section, we provide detailed statistical analysis of the dataset used for the Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage. The dataset comprises = 32, 049 video samples, specifically curated to optimize temporal reasoning and grounding capabilities through diverse task types and semantic domains. Task Type Composition. As illustrated in Figure 7, the dataset follows specific distribution across different task types to balance reward stability and semantic understanding. The majority of the dataset is dedicated to Temporal Grounding (53%). This high proportion is designed to leverage the objective nature of Temporal Intersection over Union (TIoU) metrics, which provide dense and stable reward signals during the policy optimization process. To ensure the model maintains robust logical reasoning capabilities beyond pure localization, the dataset includes substantial portion of Visual Question Answering tasks, specifically Spatial VQA (21%) and Reasoning VQA (20%). These tasks require the model to interpret visual relationships and perform causal deductions. The remaining portion consists of Temporal VQA (3%) and captioning tasks, including Global Captioning (2%) and Local Captioning (1%), which serve as auxiliary supervision to maintain general descriptive fluency. Video Duration Distribution. The temporal characteristics of the RL dataset are visualized in Figure ?? (Left). The video durations exhibit wide distribution, with mean length of 109.4 seconds and median of 123.6 seconds. significant portion of the videos is concentrated in the 100s to 200s range, providing sufficient temporal context for complex event unfolding while remaining computationally efficient for the iterative sampling required in RL. The presence of longer videos (up to 800 seconds) further challenges the models ability to maintain long-term causal consistency within the thinking process. Topic Diversity. Figure 8 (Right) presents the semantic distribution across the dataset. The collection is dominated by action-rich and logically structured domains, with Tutorial (7, 043 samples) and Sports (5, 746 samples) being the most prevalent categories. These topics are particularly suitable for RL as they often contain explicit step-by-step procedures and clear goal-oriented behaviors. Additionally, diverse array of categories such as Vlog (3, 738), Daily Life (2, 847), Cooking (2, 714), and Travel (2, 454) ensures that the models reasoning capabilities generalize across various real-world scenarios, ranging from highly structured instructional content to open-ended human activities. B.4. Data Curation Prompts This section describes the prompts used in our data curation pipeline. We employ three specialized prompts for different stages: Facts Generation, Quality Judge, and Thinking Generation. The complete prompts are illustrated in Figure 9, Figure 10, and Figure 11, respectively. Facts Generation Prompt. The Facts Generation prompt (Figure 9) instructs the model to analyze video content and produce structured summaries for pre-defined time segments. Given list of timestamps as input, the model generates Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Figure 7. Distribution of Task Types in RL Training Data. The dataset is heavily weighted towards Temporal Grounding (53%) to utilize precise IoU-based rewards, while Spatial and Reasoning VQA (combined 41%) are included to enforce high-level semantic comprehension. Figure 8. Distribution of Task Types in RL Training Data. The dataset is heavily weighted towards Temporal Grounding (53%) to utilize precise IoU-based rewards, while Spatial and Reasoning VQA (combined 41%) are included to enforce high-level semantic comprehension. theme summary and event factuals for each segment. The prompt enforces strict schema with six required components per segment: [person], [human action], [scene], [object], [camera], and [casual event caption]. critical dependency rule ensures logical consistency: if no person is present in the scene, the human action field must be marked as None. The prompt also specifies that timestamps must be preserved in their original seconds format without conversion to MM:SS, and all output must be enclosed within <facts></facts> tags. Quality Judge Prompt. The Quality Judge prompt (Figure 10) defines strict evaluation protocol for validating generated facts. It takes two inputs: the target timestamps that must be present and the model-generated structured text. The evaluation criteria are organized into four categories: (1) Format Adherence ensures proper tag structure, required headers, and all six mandatory fields in every line; (2) Timestamp Alignment verifies one-to-one correspondence between output lines and input segments, rejecting any missing or hallucinated timestamps; (3) Logical Consistency enforces the person-action dependency rule, ensuring that None person entries always correspond to None actions; and (4) Content Quality assesses completeness, fluency of captions, and specificity of descriptions. The judge outputs structured JSON containing numeric score (0-10), regeneration flag, list of critical errors, and qualitative feedback. Thinking Generation Prompt. The Thinking Generation prompt (Figure 11) guides the model to produce evidence-based reasoning chains for video question answering. Given user question and extracted factuals, the model must derive answers 15 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 through two-stage structured reasoning process. In Stage 1: Global Search & Localization, the model scans factual descriptions for keywords related to the question, identifies candidate time segments based solely on textual evidence, and explicitly states which factual IDs are selected as candidates. In Stage 2: Causal Verification, the model validates candidates using antecedent-action-consequence logic: [Antecedent] establishes the pre-condition contrast, [Verifying Key Visual Actions] provides textual proof of the event, and [Consequence] confirms the post-condition result. The output follows <thinking>...</thinking><answer>...</answer> format, ensuring that reasoning is grounded in explicit factual evidence rather than implicit assumptions. C. Instruction Tuning Data Format To effectively train the model to reason over structured evidence, we design progressive prompt engineering strategy that guides the generation of training data across three distinct stages. This multi-stage approach ensures the model first learns to extract high-density visual facts, then adapts to strict structural format, and finally integrates these skills into coherent reasoning chain. In the initial stage, the objective is to align the models visual perception with our structured event schema, as displayed in Figure 12. We explicitly instruct the model to localize activity events and generate detailed report containing global theme and detailed event factuals. We enforce rigid output format for each temporal segment, requiring specific fields such as time, person, human action, scene, object, camera, and casual event caption. This design forces the model to decompose complex video dynamics into discrete, verifiable atomic elements rather than generating generic captions, effectively transforming raw video data into the structured representation used throughout the pipeline. Before tackling complex reasoning tasks, the model must internalize the specific XML-like tag structure required for our framework. We therefore introduce format warm-up stage, as shown in Figure 13. The system prompt defines the interaction protocol: the assistant must first output the factual block, followed by thinking block, and conclude with the answering block. Since the task here is simply to reproduce video facts, the thinking block is populated with trivial placeholder. This step ensures the model adheres to the syntactic structure without the cognitive load of complex problem-solving. The final stage focuses on generating high-quality chain-of-thought data, as depicted in Figure 14. We combine the structured facts from the first stage with challenging QA pairs. The prompt requires the model to first recall the structured factual context. Crucially, the subsequent thinking block is no longer trivial; it contains detailed derivation process where the model performs global search, causal verification, and alignment based on the provided facts. This stage produces the gold-standard thinking data used to warm-start the policy model, ensuring that the reasoning process is explicitly grounded in the extracted evidence. D. More details of Reinforcement Learning D.1. Detailed Rewards Design To guide the model toward generating structured, causally grounded, and concise reasoning chains, we design composite reward function consisting of three distinct components: Format Reward, Linear IoU Reward, Multi-choice Accuracy Reward, and Length Reward. Causal Thinking Format Reward (rfmt). To enforce the structured reasoning schema proposed in our method, we design rule-based format reward. This reward evaluates whether the model strictly follows the <factual>, <thinking>, and <answering> XML structure and whether the <thinking> block explicitly traverses the required causal reasoning steps. Let be the set of mandatory causal keywords defined as: = {Global Search, Causal Verification, Final Alignment, Antecedent, Visual Verification, Consequence} (6) Let Pbasic denote the satisfaction of the basic regex pattern, Tvalid denote the constraint that all XML tags appear exactly once, and Cthink represent the content extracted from the <thinking> block. The format reward rfmt is strictly defined as: 16 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML rfmt = if Pbasic Tvalid (K Cthink) 1.0 0.5 if Pbasic [Tvalid (K Cthink)] 0.0 otherwise (pattern mismatch) (7) This reward scheme assigns baseline score of 0.5 for adhering to the basic format structure, grants full score of 1.0 only when the reasoning chain is structurally complete and causally rigorous, and assigns 0.0 for format violations. Task-Specific Performance Rewards (rtask). Depending on the downstream task type, we apply different performance metrics as rewards: Linear IoU Reward): For temporal grounding tasks, we compute the Temporal Intersection over Union (TIoU) between the predicted time segments Spred and ground truth segments Sgt. To handle ambiguity where single continuous prediction covers multiple disjoint ground truth segments, we implement hybrid scoring mechanism. Specifically, if Spred = 1 and Sgt > 1, the reward is defined as max(Coverage Ratio, Span IoU), ensuring the model is rewarded for correctly identifying the overall event span. Otherwise, we compute the average IoU across all segment pairs. Multi-choice Accuracy Reward: For QA tasks, we strictly parse the predicted option (A/B/C/D) from the <answering> block. The reward is binary indicator function: racc = I(Pred = GT). Length Reward (rlen). To prevent the verbosity tax often associated with Chain-of-Thought reasoning, we introduce soft length constraint. Unlike hard truncation, we employ piecewise linear decay function to gently discourage excessive token generation beyond target budget. Let be the generated response length, Lmax be the hard maximum length, and Lbuf er be soft buffer zone. The target length is defined as Ltarget = Lmax Lbuf er. The efficiency reward is calculated as: rlen(L) = 1.0 1.0 LLtarget Lbuf er 0.0 if Ltarget if Ltarget < Lmax if > Lmax (8) This design provides full reward for concise answers within the target budget and applies linear penalty only when the model enters the buffer zone, preventing the optimization instability caused by abrupt reward cliffs. D.2. Frank-Wolfe Algorithm for Solving the Minimum-Norm Problem To solve the minimum-norm optimization problem in Eq. (3), we employ the Frank-Wolfe algorithm, also known as the conditional gradient method (Jaggi, 2013), which is particularly well-suited for constrained convex optimization over polytopes such as the probability simplex. Problem Formulation. Recall that our objective is to find the optimal weight vector α that minimizes the squared norm of the aggregated direction in the standardized space: α = arg min αM (α), where (α) = (cid:13) ˆDqα (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:13) . This is convex quadratic program over the simplex = {α RM (cid:80)M m=1 αm = 1, αm 0}. Algorithm Description. Starting from uniform initialization α(0) = 1M , each iteration proceeds as follows: Step 1: Gradient Computation. We compute the gradient of the objective at the current iterate: (α(t)) = 2 ˆD ( ˆDqα(t)). Step 2: Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO). We solve linear minimization problem over the simplex: s(t) = arg min sM s, (α(t)). 17 (9) (10) (11) Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML Since is simplex, the solution is simply one-hot vector placing all mass on the coordinate with the smallest gradient component: s(t) = ek , where = arg min [f (α(t))]m. (12) Step 3: Exact Line Search. We compute the update direction d(t) = s(t) α(t) and determine the optimal step size via exact line search. For the quadratic objective, this admits closed-form solution: (cid:32) (cid:32) γ(t) = max 0, min 1, ˆDqα(t), ˆDqd(t) ˆDqd(t)2 (cid:33)(cid:33) . If ˆDqd(t)2 < ϵ (we use ϵ = 1012), we terminate early to avoid numerical instability. Step 4: Weight Update. We update the weights via: α(t+1) = α(t) + γ(t)d(t). (13) (14) Termination Criteria. The algorithm terminates when the change in weights falls below tolerance threshold α(t+1) α(t) < τ (we use τ = 106), or when the maximum number of iterations is reached (we use Tmax = 50). Convergence Guarantees. The exact line search ensures monotonic decrease of the objective value at every iteration, guaranteeing convergence to the global optimum of this convex problem. In the context of multi-objective optimization, the solution corresponds to Pareto-stationary point. 18 Submission and Formatting Instructions for ICML 2026 Figure 9.
